title: Preeclampsia & Eclampsia
objectives: Define preeclampsia and distinguish from gestational hypertension
Manage preeclampsia including magnesium sulfate administration

vignette.text:
A 28-year-old G1P0 at 34 weeks gestation presents with headache and visual changes. She was diagnosed with gestational hypertension at her last prenatal visit 1 week ago.

Past Medical History: No prior pregnancies, no chronic medical conditions

Medications: Prenatal vitamins

Social History: Non-smoker, no alcohol or drug use

physicalExam.abdomen: Gravid, fundal height appropriate, RUQ tenderness to palpation
physicalExam.neurologic: Hyperreflexia with 3+ reflexes, 2 beats of clonus

questions.question: Which finding is required for the diagnosis of preeclampsia?
questions.explanation: Preeclampsia requires new-onset hypertension (≥140/90) after 20 weeks PLUS either proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction. Proteinuria is no longer required if other severe features are present.

questions.question: This patient has preeclampsia with severe features. Which of the following is NOT a severe feature?
questions.explanation: Edema, while common in preeclampsia, is NOT a diagnostic criterion or severe feature. Severe features include: BP ≥160/110, thrombocytopenia <100k, elevated transaminases 2x, creatinine >1.1, pulmonary edema, new headache unresponsive to meds, or visual symptoms.

questions.question: What is the primary pathophysiologic mechanism in preeclampsia?
options: Abnormal placentation leading to endothelial dysfunction
questions.explanation: Preeclampsia results from abnormal trophoblast invasion of spiral arteries → placental ischemia → release of anti-angiogenic factors (sFlt-1) → systemic endothelial dysfunction affecting all organ systems.

questions.question: What is the definitive treatment for preeclampsia?
options: Delivery of the fetus and placenta
questions.explanation: Delivery is the only definitive treatment. The decision to deliver balances maternal risks against fetal prematurity. At 34+ weeks with severe features, delivery is typically indicated after maternal stabilization.

questions.question: What is the primary indication for magnesium sulfate in preeclampsia?
questions.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is for SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS in severe preeclampsia. It reduces eclampsia risk by ~50%. It also provides fetal neuroprotection but its primary indication is preventing maternal seizures.

questions.question: What is the target blood pressure in severe preeclampsia?
questions.explanation: Goal is to prevent stroke by keeping BP <160/110. First-line agents: IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, or PO nifedipine. Avoid overcorrection which can compromise placental perfusion.

keyTakeaways: Preeclampsia = HTN after 20 weeks + proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction
Severe features: BP ≥160/110, plt <100k, LFTs >2x, Cr >1.1, neuro symptoms
Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (not BP control)
Delivery is the only definitive treatment
HELLP syndrome: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
